title: Cross-Entropy Loss
concept: Cross-Entropy Loss measures the performance of a classification model whose output is a probability value between 0 and 1.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What function do we use to turn logits into probabilities before computing classification loss?
exercises.explain: Correct! Softmax generates the probabilities that Cross-Entropy loss evaluates.

cards.title: The cost of being confidently wrong
cards.body: If you guess an answer on a quiz and you're unsure, it's not too bad if you get it wrong. But if you loudly claim you are 99% certain and get it completely wrong, you look silly. Cross-Entropy penalizes models in the same way.

cards.title: What is Cross-Entropy Loss?
cards.body: Cross-Entropy Loss (or log loss) measures the distance between the model's predicted probability distribution and the true target distribution (which has 1.0 for the correct class and 0.0 for others). It calculates -log(p) where p is the predicted probability of the correct class.

cards.title: An elastic band
cards.body: Think of the loss as an elastic band pulling the predicted probability towards 1.0. If the prediction is close to 1.0, the band is relaxed (low loss). If the prediction is close to 0.0, the band is stretched extremely tight (huge loss).

cards.title: Loss calculation values
cards.body: If the true class is 'Dog' and the model predicts 'Dog' with probability 0.9, the loss is -log(0.9) ≈ 0.10. If the model predicts 'Dog' with probability 0.1, the loss is -log(0.1) ≈ 2.30 (much larger!).

cards.title: Loss is not just binary error
cards.body: You might think the loss just checks if the model got the prediction correct. In fact, Cross-Entropy is continuous: it pushes the model to not just get the correct class right, but to do so with high confidence.

cards.title: Cross-Entropy Loss Curve
cards.body: How loss explodes as predicted probability of the correct class drops to 0:

exercises.prompt: If a classification model predicts the correct class with 100% probability (p = 1.0), what is the Cross-Entropy loss?
exercises.explain: -log(1.0) is exactly 0. There is no error when the model is perfectly confident and correct.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Cross-Entropy loss increases as the predicted probability of the correct class decreases.
exercises.explain: True. The formula -log(p) yields larger values as p gets closer to 0.
The loss is calculated as -log(p_i) for the target class i.
exercises.sentence: Cross-Entropy loss uses the negative ___ of the predicted probability of the true class.

exercises.prompt: Which of these loss values indicates the most confident and correct model prediction?
exercises.explain: A lower loss value (like 0.05) indicates the model's predicted probability for the correct class is very close to 1.0.

exercises.prompt: Why does Cross-Entropy loss penalize a predicted probability of 0.01 on a correct class far more than it rewards a probability of 0.99?

options.text: Because the logarithm function curves sharply downward near 0, penalizing incorrect confidence exponentially.
Because the derivative of the loss is constant.
Because correct predictions don't need any training signals.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The logarithmic curve causes the loss to grow toward infinity as the predicted probability of the correct class approaches 0. This forces the model to avoid confident mistakes. Next, let's explore the limits of assuming only one class exists!
